key_insight: A two-step seismic workflow combining matched field processing and source relocation can map crevasse opening at metre-scale resolution and estimate how fast surface fractures propagate.
engineering_relevance: The method provides a practical way to quantify brittle-to-viscous fracture behavior in glaciers, improving understanding of damage evolution, meltwater routing, and ice stability.
method: Use matched field processing to detect cryoseismic events and distinguish surface icequakes from meltwater noise.
Relocate discrete arrival-time picks to refine source positions despite sparse or irregular sensor coverage.
Estimate crevasse propagation rate and diffusion coefficients from the localized event sequence.
evidence_signal: Applied to Hansbreen, Svalbard, the workflow revealed ongoing crevasse opening episodes and produced diffusion coefficients of 0.47 to 0.55 m2/s.
watch_item: Performance depends on accurate source relocation under limited instrumentation, so sparse-array geometry and picking uncertainty may still constrain robustness.
paper_title: Surface Crevasse Evolution Observed Using Matched Field Processing and Source Relocation at Hansbreen, Svalbard
